Page Turner

The Magazine for Serious Readers — Summer Issue

Annual Feature

The Summer Reading List

Twenty-seven books across three immortal genres — chosen to carry you
from the first warm evening to the last long weekend of the season.

Curated by the Editors of Page Turner

Every summer we ask ourselves the same question: what do we actually want to read when the days are long and the stakes, for once, feel low? The answer, year after year, is books that pull. Books that keep you up past the time you meant to stop. Books dense enough to reward slow afternoons and propulsive enough to survive the beach.

This year's list is organized around three genres we believe are underrepresented on the usual summer tables: classic science fiction, gothic and horror, and hardboiled detective fiction. Each has its own pleasure, its own pace, and its own way of telling the truth slant.

We have included a brief synopsis for each title. These are not spoiler-free plot summaries. They are arguments for why the book deserves your next three days.

Start anywhere. There is no wrong order. There is only the next unread page.

Part One

Classic Science Fiction

The canon that invented tomorrow — nine novels that asked the questions civilization is still catching up to.

Title Author Synopsis
Dune Frank Herbert On the desert planet Arrakis, young Paul Atreides navigates political intrigue, betrayal, and messianic destiny amid the struggle to control the universe's most precious resource. As layered and unforgiving as the sand it describes.
Foundation Isaac Asimov A mathematician foresees the collapse of a galactic empire and races to preserve human knowledge through the dark age that will follow. The first volume of a series that shaped every space opera that came after it.
The Left Hand of Darkness Ursula K. Le Guin A human envoy visits a world whose inhabitants have no fixed gender, and the friendship he slowly forges with a disgraced politician across a frozen waste is one of the most moving relationships in all of science fiction.
Brave New World Aldous Huxley In a society engineered for happiness, where history is forbidden and discomfort has been abolished, one man's desire for truth becomes an act of radical defiance -- and a tragedy the world will not pause to mourn.
The War of the Worlds H.G. Wells Martian war-machines reduce Victorian England to rubble in hours, and one ordinary man chronicles humanity's panicked, helpless retreat. Reads faster than it has any right to at 130 years old.
Fahrenheit 451 Ray Bradbury In a future where firemen start fires rather than extinguish them, a book-burner begins to wonder what's inside the things he destroys. Bradbury's prose burns as hot as the pages it depicts.
The Stars My Destination Alfred Bester Gully Foyle is a nobody stranded in space and left to die by a passing ship. He survives on pure hatred, stages one of fiction's great vendettas, and becomes something far more frightening than a villain.
More Than Human Theodore Sturgeon Six misfits -- each fragmentary, each broken -- discover that together they form a gestalt organism with powers none of them possesses alone. A novel about belonging that doubles as one of the genre's strangest and most tender achievements.
A Canticle for Leibowitz Walter M. Miller Jr. A desert monastery preserves the scattered relics of lost knowledge across three thousand years of civilization's collapse and painful recovery. Funny, bleak, and quietly devastating in equal measure.

Part Two

Gothic & Horror

Fear as a lens — nine novels that use dread to illuminate what sunlit fiction politely ignores.

Title Author Synopsis
Frankenstein Mary Shelley A scientist creates life and immediately abandons it. The creature's anguished search for compassion -- and his turn toward destruction when none is offered -- makes this one of literature's most devastating arguments about responsibility.
Dracula Bram Stoker Told entirely through letters, diaries, and newspaper clippings, the count's invasion of England unfolds as a breathless Victorian thriller laced with genuine menace. The epistolary form makes you feel like you're piecing together a catastrophe in real time.
The Picture of Dorian Gray Oscar Wilde A beautiful young man trades his soul for eternal youth and watches a hidden portrait absorb every sin while his face stays pristine. Wilde's only novel, and it contains more aphorisms per page than most writers manage in a career.
The Turn of the Screw Henry James A governess in a remote English manor becomes convinced her two angelic charges are haunted by the ghosts of former servants. Or has she simply lost her mind? After a hundred and twenty-five years, James still will not tell you.
Rebecca Daphne du Maurier The new wife of a wealthy widower moves into his magnificent estate and discovers that the shadow of his first wife, the dazzling Rebecca, has never left -- and never intends to. The suspense is architectural; it builds in every room.
The Haunting of Hill House Shirley Jackson Four strangers spend a summer in a house that was, Jackson tells us in the first paragraph, born bad. One of them will not be the same person who arrived. Opens with one of the finest first paragraphs in American fiction.
We Have Always Lived in the Castle Shirley Jackson Merricat Blackwood and her sister live peacefully in their ancestral home, shunned by the village for reasons the novel reveals slowly and devastatingly. Told from inside one of fiction's most alarming and oddly lovable minds.
Something Wicked This Way Comes Ray Bradbury A sinister carnival arrives in a small Midwestern town one October, and two thirteen-year-old boys discover that some rides steal years from your life. A novel about fathers and sons as much as it is about evil.
House of Leaves Mark Z. Danielewski A scholarly apparatus -- footnotes, appendices, color-coded text -- surrounds a documentary about a house that is larger on the inside than the outside. Designed to be disorienting. Succeeds completely. Do not begin it on a night you plan to sleep.

Part Three

Hardboiled Detective Fiction

The mean streets, the missing persons, the lies inside the lies — nine novels that made cynicism into an art form.

Title Author Synopsis
The Big Sleep Raymond Chandler Philip Marlowe takes a case for a dying general and walks into a Los Angeles underworld of blackmail and murder where every solution uncovers two new problems. The plot famously doesn't fully resolve, and it doesn't need to.
The Maltese Falcon Dashiell Hammett When Sam Spade's partner is killed while tailing a client, the trail leads to a priceless jeweled bird and every kind of liar. The novel that established the private eye as a moral figure in an immoral world.
Farewell, My Lovely Raymond Chandler Marlowe is pulled into a search for an ex-con's missing girlfriend and ends up entangled in jewel theft, a corrupt cop, and a society murder. Many critics consider it Chandler's finest construction, and they are not wrong.
The Long Goodbye Raymond Chandler A chance friendship with a charming drunk leads Marlowe into a case that strips away every comfortable illusion about loyalty, wealth, and love. Longer, slower, and more melancholy than the earlier books -- the one where Chandler lets himself be sad.
Red Harvest Dashiell Hammett The Continental Op arrives in a company-owned mining town so thoroughly corrupt that the only way to clean it out is to play every faction against the others until they destroy each other. Lean, violent, and morally uncomfortable.
I, the Jury Mickey Spillane Mike Hammer's best friend is murdered and Hammer vows to deliver the punishment himself, not through a courtroom but with his own hand. Spillane wrote this in nine days. It feels like it, in the best possible sense.
The Moving Target Ross Macdonald Lew Archer is hired to find a missing millionaire and discovers a California family rotting from the inside out. The first Archer novel and an immediate argument that Macdonald deserved to stand alongside Hammett and Chandler.
The Chill Ross Macdonald A young husband hires Archer to find his runaway bride, and the trail leads back through two decades of buried crimes toward a secret someone will kill again to protect. The novel in which Macdonald's method -- archaeology as detective work -- reaches its fullest expression.
Devil in a Blue Dress Walter Mosley Easy Rawlins, an unemployed black veteran in 1948 Los Angeles, is hired to find a white woman who likes jazz clubs on Central Avenue, and stumbles into something far more dangerous -- and more revealing about postwar America -- than anyone warned him.

Twenty-seven books. Twenty-seven arguments that fiction, at its best, is not an escape from the world but a way of seeing it more clearly. Science fiction imagines the consequences of ideas before the world has to live them. Gothic and horror name the fears that polite conversation refuses to acknowledge. Hardboiled detective fiction insists that someone has to go looking for the truth even when everyone else has agreed to stop.

These are not comfort reads, exactly. But they are the kind of books you finish and immediately want to press into someone else's hands. That impulse -- the need to make someone else read the thing you just read -- is the closest thing to a definition of a great book that we have ever been able to agree on here at Page Turner.

We hope at least one title on this list does that to you. We hope it ruins your sleep, monopolizes your commute, and makes the end of summer feel like a small loss. See you in the fall.

Page Turner Magazine — Summer Issue Books • Culture • Criticism pagerturnermag.example.com
